choices.text: Containers share the host kernel; a compromise as root makes container escape and host compromise dramatically easier. Non-root + dropped capabilities + read-only FS restrict post-compromise actions. Standard production hardening: USER directive in Dockerfile, --cap-drop=ALL at runtime, --read-only with tmpfs for needed writable paths.

questions.explanation: Non-root + minimal capabilities + read-only = smaller blast radius on compromise.

questions.prompt: Where should image vulnerability scanning happen in CI/CD?

choices.text: Multiple points; CI is the highest-leverage. In CI: fail builds on Critical CVEs before image push. In the registry: continuous scans of pushed images so new CVEs alert. At deploy: admission controllers verify signatures and scan status. At runtime: monitor deployed images for newly-disclosed CVEs. Tools: Trivy, Grype, Snyk, Docker Scout, registry-native scanners.

questions.explanation: Scan in CI to fail fast; in registry for ongoing visibility; verify at deploy. Multi-point defense.

questions.prompt: Why reference production images by digest rather than mutable tags?

choices.text: Tags can be retagged silently — supply chain attack risk or human error. Digests (sha256:...) are immutable content hashes; same digest always means same content. Production manifests reference digests; humans use tags for readability. Combined with Cosign signing and admission controllers verifying signatures, you get strong supply-chain integrity.

questions.explanation: Digests immutable; tags mutable. Production references = digests.

questions.prompt: K8s cluster deploys fail with Docker Hub 'too many requests' errors. Fix?

choices.text: Pull-through cache or mirror. Docker Hub limits anonymous pulls (100 per 6h) and authenticated free pulls (200) per IP. Production clusters hit these during scale-up events. Mitigation: deploy a pull-through cache (ECR Public, Harbor, distribution registry) in the cluster's region; configure containerd to use it; cached pulls don't hit Docker Hub. Or mirror base images to your own registry on a schedule. Or pay for Docker Hub. For new cloud K8s deployments, use the cloud's native registry (ECR/GAR/ACR) for application images and a cache for upstream images.

questions.explanation: Pull-through cache or mirrors avoid Docker Hub rate limits.

questions.prompt: Three tools for a quick image audit?

choices.text: `docker history image` (layers, sizes, instructions — surfaces bloat), `dive image` (interactive layer browser — reveals leftover artifacts, missing cleanup, accidental secrets), `trivy image image` (vulnerability scan — known CVEs with severity). Run all three on any image to surface most common issues within minutes. For runtime check, `docker inspect` shows configured user and security flags.

questions.explanation: history + dive + trivy is the quick image audit kit.
